does: This paper introduces the first public multi-modal dataset of 100 Turkish phone call conversations (audio and transcripts) paired with scam/benign labels, and systematically evaluates seven large language models across three model families on this detection task under different input conditions. The authors test whether LLMs can identify phone scams by processing raw audio, automatic speech-to-text transcripts, and human-corrected transcripts. Key findings show that transcript-based approaches substantially outperform direct audio processing, while manually refined transcripts perform comparably to automatic transcriptions, suggesting that automatic speech recognition may be sufficiently reliable for this application. The work establishes baseline performance metrics for Turkish scam detection using current state-of-the-art models.
connects: This research responds to a significant gap in multilingual AI safety and scam detection research, which has concentrated overwhelmingly on English and other high-resource languages despite global prevalence of phone fraud targeting non-English speaking communities. The work builds on emerging efforts to extend language models and detection systems to low-resource languages, combining insights from speech processing, natural language understanding, and fraud detection. It leverages recent advances in multimodal large language models that can process both audio and text, extending their application from typical benchmarks to real-world security threats. The dataset creation methodology draws on practices in computational sociolinguistics and community-centered ML research that emphasize collecting authentic data from underserved populations.
enables: This foundational dataset and benchmark enable future work on culturally adapted scam detection systems for Turkish speakers and other low-resource language communities. The systematic comparison of input modalities informs practical deployment decisions for real-time protection systems operating under varying technical constraints. This work points toward building culturally aware AI safety systems that account for language-specific social engineering tactics and local scam patterns. It opens avenues for studying how LLM performance on safety-critical tasks degrades in lower-resource settings, potentially motivating model development and fine-tuning approaches tailored to underserved populations. The framework could seed similar efforts in other vulnerable languages, ultimately enabling more equitable distribution of technological defenses against phone fraud across the global population.
